questions.title: Explain the event loop.
questions.shortAnswer: JS runs on a single thread. The event loop pulls callbacks from the microtask queue (Promises) and then the macrotask queue (setTimeout, I/O) when the call stack is empty.
questions.detailedExplanation: Each tick: run all microtasks until exhausted, render if needed, then take one macrotask. This is why a Promise resolution runs before a 0ms setTimeout.
questions.realWorldExample: A heavy synchronous loop blocks rendering because the event loop can't pick up paint or input tasks until the stack clears.
bestPractices: Break long work with setTimeout or scheduler.yield
commonMistakes: Blocking the loop with sync work
questions.interviewTip: Mention microtask vs macrotask explicitly — it's the canonical distinction.

questions.title: What's the difference between == and ===?
questions.shortAnswer: == performs type coercion before comparing; === requires both type and value to match.
questions.detailedExplanation: Coercion rules are unintuitive (e.g. [] == false). Always prefer === unless you specifically want loose equality (rare).
questions.realWorldExample: Lint configs (eslint:recommended) enable eqeqeq by default.
bestPractices: Use == null only as a null/undefined check

questions.title: Closures — what are they?
questions.shortAnswer: A closure is a function that retains access to variables from its lexical scope even after that scope has exited.
questions.detailedExplanation: Every function in JS captures its surrounding scope. This enables private state, factories, and partial application.
questions.realWorldExample: useState's setter is a closure capturing the React internal state slot.
bestPractices: Be aware of memory: closures hold references
commonMistakes: Capturing loop var with var instead of let
questions.interviewTip: Connect closures to React hook stale-state bugs — modern, practical.

questions.title: var vs let vs const.
questions.shortAnswer: var is function-scoped and hoisted; let and const are block-scoped. const prevents reassignment (not deep immutability).
questions.detailedExplanation: let/const live in the temporal dead zone until declared. const arrays/objects can still be mutated — Object.freeze for shallow immutability.
questions.realWorldExample: Use const by default, let when reassignment is needed, never var in new code.
questions.interviewTip: Mention TDZ by name — shows depth.

questions.title: How does prototypal inheritance work?
questions.shortAnswer: Every object has a [[Prototype]] link. Property lookups walk the chain until found or null is reached.
questions.detailedExplanation: class syntax is sugar over prototypes. Object.create(proto) sets the prototype directly. Methods defined on a class live on Class.prototype, shared across instances.
questions.realWorldExample: Array methods like .map live on Array.prototype, not on each array instance.
